name: Almoharín from Miajadas – circular by the Canal de Orellana
name_original: Almoharín desde Miajadas – circular por la Canal de Orellana
description: This difficult 27.5-mile touring cycling route from Miajadas explores agricultural fields and the Canal de Orellana in Extremadura.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Miajadas offers routes through a landscape characterized by agricultural plains, river valleys, and historical sites. The region features routes along the Canal de Orellana and the Burdalo River, providing varied terrain for cyclists. Elevation gains on some routes indicate rolling hills, particularly around areas like Montánchez Castle. Best touring cycling routes around Miajadas The most popular touring cycling route is Almoharín from Miajadas – circular by the Canal de Orellana, a 27.5 miles (44.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 17 minutes to complete. This difficult route follows the Canal de Orellana, offering a distinct waterside cycling experience. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Burdalo River Route loop from Miajadas, a difficult 29.8 miles (48.0 km) path. This route explores the scenery around the Burdalo River, featuring a mix of natural and agricultural landscapes. Local touring cyclists also love the Montánchez Castle – Plaza de España, Montánchez loop from Miajadas, a 52.0 miles (83.7 km) trail leading through historical areas and hilly terrain, often completed in about 7 hours 46 minutes. Touring cycling around Miajadas is defined by river paths, canal routes, and historical landmarks like Montánchez Castle. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various skill levels. The routes in Miajadas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 80 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Miajadas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Miajadas?
A: There are 17 touring cycling routes around Miajadas, offering a variety of experiences across different difficulty levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Miajadas?
A: The routes primarily traverse agricultural plains and river valleys, such as those along the Canal de Orellana and the Burdalo River. Some areas, particularly around Montánchez Castle, feature rolling hills.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes in Miajadas?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527268" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Silos of Miajadas loop from Miajadas&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate circular route.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Miajadas?
A: Yes, Miajadas offers 5 easy touring cycling routes. These routes typically have less elevation gain and are shorter in distance, making them ideal for beginners or those seeking a relaxed ride.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes in Miajadas?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, there are 7 difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31846337" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montánchez Castle – Plaza de España, Montánchez loop from Miajadas&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 52-mile (83.7 km) route with significant elevation changes, offering a strenuous ride through historical areas.

Q: What is the best season for touring cycling in Miajadas?
A: The best seasons for touring cycling in Miajadas are typically spring and autumn when temperatures are milder and the landscape is vibrant. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cooler and wetter, though cycling is possible year-round.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Miajadas?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' routes are not explicitly categorized, the 5 easy routes available would be the most suitable for families, offering gentler terrain and shorter distances. Always check the route details for specific suitability.

Q: What interesting landmarks or attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Along the routes, you can discover several points of interest. Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5580811" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santiago Apóstol and Tomato Sculpture of Miajadas&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5966896" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruecas River in Palazuelo&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5709687" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parish Church of El Salvador, Almoharín&lt;/a&gt;. The routes also pass by the historic Montánchez Castle.

Q: Are there viewpoints or scenic spots on the Miajadas cycling routes?
A: Yes, routes often offer scenic views, particularly along the Canal de Orellana and the Burdalo River, showcasing the region's natural and agricultural landscapes. The area around Montánchez Castle also provides elevated perspectives.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Miajadas?
A: The touring cycling routes in Miajadas are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from river paths and canal routes to historical landmarks, providing a diverse and engaging experience.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads for touring cycling routes in Miajadas?
A: Miajadas, being a town, generally offers public parking options. For specific trailheads, it's advisable to check local maps or route descriptions on komoot for designated parking areas near the starting points of your chosen route.

Q: Can I access touring cycling routes in Miajadas using public transport?
A: Miajadas is connected by regional bus services, which may allow access to the town. However, public transport options directly to specific trailheads outside the town center might be limited. It's best to research local bus schedules and policies regarding bicycles if you plan to use public transport.
tips: On the southwestern edge of town, you can find the old silo building that used to house large amounts of grain collected from the local fields. Impressive great agricultural cathedrals.
Large gravel roads along the irrigation canal give life to the agricultural fields that surround this area of Miajadas. Walk along the service roads of the canal.

Almoharín is a small town in the south of the province of Cáceres in the region of Montánchez. Surrounded by agricultural fields, the scenic region is known for its fig production. Ride a bike through the city, head to Plaza España and admire the Parochial Church of El Salvador.
The great parish church of Santiago de Miajadas is an impressive monument. The origin of the religious site dates back to 1348 although the church you see today was built later in the Middle Ages.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This touring bicycle route is approximately 44.3 km long and generally takes around 3 hours and 17 minutes to complete. However, as it's considered a difficult ride, your actual time may vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and is considered a hard bike ride. It requires good fitness, and some segments may be unpaved and challenging, potentially requiring you to push your bike. It is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is mixed and varied. You can expect to encounter agricultural fields, dirt roads, sand tracks, and some paved sections. While specific gradient details for this exact route are limited, the broader Orellana area features varied terrain with notable elevation changes in parts.

faqs.question: What landmarks and natural features can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse sights. You'll pass by the impressive Church of Santiago de Miajadas and the Silos of Miajadas. A significant part of the journey skirts the Canal de Orellana, which leads to the Orellana Reservoir, a vital natural area known for its rich birdlife and beautiful landscapes. In Almoharín, you can see the Parish Church of El Salvador.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Since the route starts and ends in Miajadas, you can look for parking options within the town. Specific parking areas for cyclists are not detailed, but general street parking should be available in Miajadas.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to eat along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through Miajadas and Almoharín, both of which are towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and other amenities. It's advisable to stock up on water and snacks before you set off, especially for the sections between towns.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in outdoor public spaces, specific rules for cycling with dogs on this route are not provided. Given the mixed terrain and potential for unpaved sections, ensure your dog is comfortable with the distance and varied surfaces. Always keep your dog under control, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near livestock.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. The Canal de Orellana and Orellana Reservoir area are part of protected natural networks, but access for cycling is generally open.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Extremadura region can experience very hot summers. Therefore, the best times to cycle this route are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) when temperatures are milder and more pleasant for outdoor activities. These seasons also offer beautiful natural scenery.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Camino Visigodo - Etapa 2 (13% of the route), Camino Visigodo - Etapa 1 (5%), Calle Molineros (2%), Calle Johann Sebastian Bach (1%), and Puente del Coto (1%).

faqs.question: What gear should I bring for this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' rating and mixed terrain, we recommend a touring bicycle suitable for varied surfaces. Essential gear includes a helmet, plenty of water, snacks, a repair kit, and appropriate clothing for the weather. Consider tires that can handle both paved and unpaved sections, as some parts may be challenging.
